macro_segments.title: The widow selling fields for sustenance: basic mechanism and dispute over duration

micro_segments.title: Introduction: how a widow sells fields belonging to the heirs
Rav Daniel bar Rav Katina citing Rav Huna: selling for twelve months with monthly payments
Rav Yehuda's more conservative position: six months selling, monthly payments
Baraitot supporting both Rav Huna and Rav Yehuda; Ameimar rules like Rav Yehuda
Rav Ashi challenges Ameimar: why reject Rav Huna? Ameimar's response: lo shmi'a lei

macro_segments.title: Liens and mortgages: can a widow seize land she sold for sustenance to pay her ketubah

micro_segments.title: Question posed to Rav Sheshet: widow sells land for sustenance; can she later seize it for ketubah?
Explanation of liens and mortgages in loans and ketubah obligations
Key distinction: ketubah is mortgaged, but mezonot cannot be seized as a lien
Citation of Rav Yosef: responsibility (achrayut) for mortgaged land falls on heirs
Two possible objections: either widow acts as agent or heirs argue she accepted responsibility
Rav Sheshet's answer: a baraita permits her to continue selling until ketubah is covered
Debate: is the baraita eitza tova (good advice) or binding din (law)?
Inference from baraita language: if she leaves fields, she can collect ketubah; if not, she cannot

macro_segments.title: General principle: can a seller rescind a sale if he no longer needs the money?

micro_segments.title: Question: if someone sells property unnecessarily, can they reverse the sale?
First example: man sells land to Rav Pappa to buy oxen, then finds oxen cheaper
Rav Pappa returns the land as an act of piety (lifnim mishurat hadin), not legal obligation
Second example: local famine in Nehardea; wheat prices crash after boats arrive
Rav Nachman rules: houses should return to original owners when artificial shortage ends
Gemara qualification: this is mekach taaut (mistaken sale) only if situation was unknowable
Rashi clarifies: boats nearby was knowable; unexpected supply from elsewhere is not
Concern: ruling may discourage future sales if buyers fear reversals
Rav Nachman responds: artificial shortages are rare in Nehardea; ruling stands
Hilcheta: seller can rescind if he no longer needs the money; buyer must repay

macro_segments.title: Widow's authority to sell without beit din: distinction between ketubah from marriage vs. engagement

micro_segments.title: Mishna: widow from marriage or engagement can sell without beit din per tanna kama
Rabbi Shimon's restriction: widow from engagement needs beit din unless she has mezonot
Gemara question: why allow widow from marriage to sell without beit din?
Ulla cites China (grace): allowing easy process encourages remarriage and pleasant memory
Rashi interpretation: women prefer pleasant memory of deceased husband
Rabbi Yochanan's alternative: no one wants his wife embarrassed in beit din
Question: what of a divorced woman (grushah)? Does she need beit din or not?
Analysis: if chen is the reason, divorced woman should also not need beit din
If lack of embarrassment is the reason, divorced woman should need beit din
Mishna introduces grushah: she cannot sell without beit din
Conclusion: grushah ruling suggests Rabbi Shimon's position and embarrassment argument

macro_segments.title: Parallel rule for heirs selling widow's ketubah property without beit din

micro_segments.title: Baraita: just as widow sells without beit din, so heirs of ketubah inherit and can sell without beit din
Analysis: if embarrassment is the reason, why would it apply to male heirs?
If chen (grace/assistance) is the reason, it should not apply to male heirs
Ulla's targumic reinterpretation: heirs mentioned are daughter or sister, who also need chen

topical_tags.term: Rav Huna vs. Rav Yehuda: duration of sale
Ketubah mortgage vs. mezonot lien distinction
Eitza tova (good advice) vs. din (binding law)
Rav Pappa and the oxen sale
Rabbi Shimon: widow from engagement needs beit din
Rashi vs. Tosafot on chen interpretation
Widow from marriage vs. engagement (ketubot difference)
